title: Quadratic Flatness and Regularity for Codimension-One Varifolds with Bounded Anisotropic Mean Curvature
speaker: Sławomir Kolasiński
abstract: Let Ω be an open set in a Euclidean space X of dimension (n+1) and ϕ be a uniformly convex smooth norm on X. Consider an n-dimensional unit-density varifold V in Ω, whose generalised mean curvature vector, computed with respect to ϕ, is bounded. Assume also that the n-dimensional Hausdorff measure restricted to the support Σ of V is absolutely continuous with respect to the weight measure of V. In my recent work with Mario Santilli (arXiv:2507.18357), we showed that there exists an open and dense subset R of Σ at points of which one can touch Σ by two mutually tangent balls. At points of R we get quadratic height decay and we then apply Allard's 1986 regularity theorem to show that these points are actually regular points of class (1,α) for any 0 less than α less than 1. We prove also that R is almost equal to a subset Σ* of points of Σ, where at least one blow-up limit (in the sense of Painlevé-Kuratowski) is not the whole space X. The condition on the blow-up limit seems very weak and does not entail any regularity a priori; hence, there is hope that in many cases Σ* almost equals Σ. In my talk I shall outline the history of the problem of regularity for varifolds satisfying bounds on anisotropic first variation and I shall present main ingredients of the proof of the above result.

proof_stages.label: Anisotropic mean curvature and NH comparison sets
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes a smooth uniformly convex norm, defines the anisotropic normal and mean-curvature vector both classically and variationally, then introduces NH sets by a touching-surface comparison principle and notes that supports of varifolds with bounded anisotropic first variation are NH sets.

proof_stages.label: Set-theoretic tangents and the main two-sided touching theorem
proof_stages.summary: Using Painlevé-Kuratowski blow-ups to define the set A* and using the proximal normal bundle to define the two-sided touching set R, the main theorem states that an NH set with locally finite touchable locus and zero Lebesgue measure has positive density of R at every point of A*, hence R covers A* up to H^n-null set.

proof_stages.label: Varifold regularity application and density-one limitation
proof_stages.summary: The theorem applies to supports of varifolds with bounded anisotropic first variation, replacing the Euclidean monotonicity-based flatness input in Allard-type regularity and giving a large or dense regular set at density-one points, while the Q&A emphasizes remaining multiplicity and support-measure assumptions.

proof_stages.label: Metric projection, reach, and generalized principal curvatures
proof_stages.summary: The proof begins with the Euclidean nearest-point projection, the unique-projection set, the normal map and reach function, and a Federer-Santilli-Hug type theorem giving almost-everywhere differentiability and well-defined generalized principal curvatures on the normal bundle.

proof_stages.label: Curvature bounds from the NH condition
proof_stages.summary: Lemma 1 converts the anisotropic NH comparison inequality into upper bounds for sums of generalized principal curvatures, complementing the reach lower bounds and ensuring the relevant curvatures are finite.

proof_stages.label: Positive measure of one-sided touchable boundary
proof_stages.summary: Lemma 2 uses disintegration of Lebesgue measure along nearest-projection fibers to show that, when the generalized curvatures are finite almost everywhere, the boundary points touchable by a ball have locally positive H^n measure.

proof_stages.label: Almost-everywhere two-sided touching
proof_stages.summary: Lemma 3 proves that under zero volume and locally finite H^n measure of the touchable locus, almost every one-sided touchable point is actually touchable from both sides, via rectifiability, a Minkowski-content contradiction, and dominated convergence using the curvature bounds.

proof_stages.label: Density at A* from blow-up holes
proof_stages.summary: Lemma 4 completes the theorem by showing that any nontrivial tangent blow-up at a point of A* contains a missing ball, and a local diffeomorphism turns this hole into a positive-density set of hyperplane- or ball-touchable, hence two-sided-touchable, nearby points.

examples.name: Euclidean norm benchmark for varifold regularity
examples.note: The standard Euclidean case, where monotonicity gives lower density and L-infinity flatness for Allard regularity, is used as a contrast showing what is missing in the anisotropic setting.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the Euclidean-norm case as a benchmark and traces the monotonicity formula through density lower bounds, tangent-plane identification, flatness, and Allard regularity consequences

passes.reason: The Euclidean norm is the maximally rotationally symmetric norm used as the positive benchmark where monotonicity and Allard regularity are available.
The Euclidean norm is the rotationally invariant model norm used as a benchmark for monotonicity and Allard regularity, not to refute a claim.
The Euclidean norm is maximally rotation-invariant and is used as the standard positive benchmark for monotonicity and regularity, not to refute a claim.
The Euclidean norm is used as the regular, isotropic benchmark and is maximally rotation-symmetric, not as a failure case.
the Euclidean norm is the maximally rotation-symmetric norm used here as the regularity benchmark, not to refute a claim
The Euclidean norm is the maximally rotationally invariant norm and is used as a positive benchmark for monotonicity and Allard regularity, not as a failure case.
the Euclidean norm is used as a positive benchmark case and is distinguished by full orthogonal symmetry
The Euclidean norm is used as the positive benchmark for monotonicity and regularity, and its unit ball has maximal rotational symmetry.
The Euclidean norm is the maximally rotation-invariant benchmark for the positive monotonicity/regularity theory, not a failure case.
The Euclidean norm is the maximally rotationally symmetric benchmark where monotonicity and Allard regularity are known, not a refuting example.
the Euclidean norm is used as the regularity benchmark and is maximally rotation-symmetric among norms.
used as the positive benchmark for regularity, with the Euclidean unit ball having maximal rotational symmetry.

examples.name: Nearest-point projection and nonunique projection sketch
examples.note: In response to a request for pictures, the speaker sketches points with unique and nonunique nearest projections to make the unique-nearest-point set and reach function concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker defines the nearest-point projection maps and then sketches points with unique and nonunique projections, reasoning through the right-angle projection picture and reach along normals

passes.reason: The sketched point with two nearest points is an ordinary medial-axis situation used to show that nearest-point projection need not be single-valued.
The sketched closed-set configuration with more than one nearest point is used to show that nearest-point projection need not be single-valued.
The sketched medial-axis/equidistant case is a singular tie where nearest projection is not single-valued, used to show uniqueness of projection can fail.
A point with two nearest points is sketched to show that nearest-point projection need not be single-valued.
a generic closed-set nearest-projection configuration is used to show that the projection need not be unique
Nearest-point projection is defined by distance minimizers, and the sketch with two equidistant closest points shows that the projection need not be single-valued.
a point with two nearest points shows global uniqueness of projection fails and is a medial-axis boundary case
A point with several nearest points is shown to demonstrate that the nearest-point projection need not be single-valued, while the configuration is otherwise ordinary.
The equidistant medial-axis point is a boundary case used to show nearest-point projection need not be unique.
A medial-axis/cut-locus point with tied nearest points is a boundary/critical case used to show nearest-point projection can be multivalued.
the nonunique nearest-point sketch shows projection need not be single-valued and lies on the medial-axis boundary where distance minimizers tie.
the sketched medial-axis point with two nearest points shows nearest-point projection need not be single-valued.

examples.name: Higher-multiplicity varifold thought experiment
examples.note: An audience question about a varifold with a given multiplicity at one point and larger multiplicity nearby illustrates the limitation of the regularity corollary to density-one or multiplicity-one hypotheses.
examples.engagement_reason: higher multiplicity is raised only as a speculative Q&A scenario about whether the argument could extend, without carrying out a construction or calculation for such a varifold

passes.reason: A higher-multiplicity varifold is a standard non-multiplicity-one case raised as an obstruction to extending the regularity/density argument.
A higher-multiplicity varifold is raised as the enemy case obstructing a direct extension of the density-one regularity argument.
A higher-multiplicity varifold is a collapsed multiple-sheet situation raised as the obstruction to removing the density-one/multiplicity-one limitation.
Higher multiplicity is raised as the adversarial case obstructing a direct extension of the density-one regularity argument.
a higher-multiplicity varifold is an ordinary integral-varifold possibility raised as a possible extension issue rather than as a constructed counterexample
A higher-multiplicity varifold is an ordinary integral-varifold possibility raised as the enemy case obstructing a direct extension of the density-one regularity argument.
higher multiplicity is raised as an obstruction to extending the density-one regularity conclusion and represents coincident weighted sheets
The higher-multiplicity varifold is raised as an obstruction to removing the multiplicity-one/density-one hypothesis, with no special intrinsic structure specified beyond higher multiplicity.
A higher-multiplicity varifold is a standard integral-varifold scenario raised as a possible extension beyond density one, not an actual disproof.
A higher-multiplicity varifold is raised as an obstruction to extending the density-one regularity argument, while such multiplicity is structurally ordinary for integral varifolds.
a higher-multiplicity varifold is raised as an adversarial case obstructing extension of the density-one regularity argument, but is otherwise an ordinary varifold phenomenon.
raised as an exploratory possible extension beyond multiplicity one, while higher multiplicity is ordinary for integral varifolds.
